data.title: Teladoc Health: Needs A Growth Catalyst
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Teladoc (NYSE:TDOC) had an incredible three months of positive momentum, driven by high optimism coming from investors and speculators. Most believe there's an overlooked AI play; this narrative came up after the company released its AI product that helps physicians monitor patients' movements remotely. Given its attractive valuation at the time, buying the stock upon that announcement was a no-brainer. The stock has rallied by 20% since that announcement. I am not as bullish; the company is still spending a lot on customer acquisition, cash reserves might decline due to Livongo's convertible notes maturing in June this year; meanwhile, revenues remain stagnant. To any conservative investor like myself, I would advise remaining on the sidelines and waiting for the revenue growth rates to improve.

Company Background

Teladoc is the largest provider of telehealth services in the United States, catering to more than 93 million members on its Integrated care services and has about 400K paying users on their Mental Health therapy platform, BetterHelp. The telemedicine space is actually not as easy to operate in a way that makes economic sense for all parties involved, namely, the patient, insurer, and physician. The patient enjoys the convenience of not having to go to the doctor's office and waiting in a queue to be assisted, but they can get all of that from the comfort of their home.

From the outside looking in, this looks like a commodity service; one just has to build a platform that connects doctors and patients. This is far from the truth; there's a Lindy effect at play here. Teladoc, as the oldest platform to provide virtual health services, is most likely to survive for a couple of reasons:

  1. Was early to recruit good, qualified physicians on its platform - Teladoc had the first-mover advantage of introducing most physicians to its platform; there's a network effect at play here; patients are most likely to trust a platform with the most qualified physicians and a broad distribution.
  2. High switching costs imbedded - Once the patients are onboarded to the platform, they are most likely to stick with it; as a patient, you want to be serviced by the same physician who serviced you previously, and also given the confidential nature of these services, no patient wants to share his health information with multiple telehealth platforms.
  3. Strong relationship with insurers - Initially, Teladoc connected patients with physicians, a direct-to-customer approach where patients paid directly out of their pocket for these virtual visits, but as the platform grew, it was easier to convince insurance companies to use their platform as an additional service to their insurance coverage package. The company reported to have more than a 90% retention rate with the insurance companies.

So, if you are building a new telehealth platform, you have to go through these multiple hops, gather trust, and have a convincing story that will convince insurers to switch from a well-trusted platform to yours. Without insurers, you cannot recruit good physicians to your platform and pay them well; without physicians, no insurer will add your platform to their insurance package. You can see how this easily turns into a death spiral for most telehealth platforms. All of this to say, Teladoc Health, ladies and gentlemen, is here to stay.

Growth Concerns

Teladoc has been experiencing stagnant revenue growth in the couple of quarters. Looking at the chart below, TDOC had incredible revenue growth at the start of 2020, which we all know was a result of a pandemic that led to a lockdown and most of the economy had to rely on remote services to keep on running. TDOC was the benefactor of that trend; the stock rallied by 285% from Dec 2019 to Feb 2021. One could argue and say the stock has reached the S-curve of its growth; from now on, going forward, the company will not grow exponentially but rather logarithmically as the revenue chart might suggest.

I am not convinced that's the case. I believe more growth is to be expected from this company as the ultimate goal is to reduce US healthcare spending, which is currently 18% of its GDP. Historically, what the company has been doing was to replace offline doctor's visits with virtual ones, bringing time and cost savings to the end customers. Now we are starting to see a divergence; there are more health services that are impossible to render physically in a cost-efficient manner, making digital services mandatory. These are services that collect data from home testing kits and make it easily accessible to the relevant physician for constant monitoring. These services that are offered within the Teladoc IC platform beyond the virtual care services.

This segment of the business is more stable and suffers fewer revenue shocks due to recurring revenue from insurance companies. In Q3 2024, TDOC increased its IC members to 94 million, a 4% increased from Q3 2023, chronic care members increased by 5%. Currently, only 1.3% of IC members get chronic care services. The company charges chronic care management services at a premium, and more growth is expected here, which will be a positive catalyst for the company.

Recent Acquisition

Earlier this month, the company announced the acquisition of Catapult Health, a startup that provides preventive care services through virtual check-ups using home testing kits. Catapult Health services will be infused within the IC segment of the business. This is a positive direction the company is taking as it adds more value to its customer base, increasing stickiness and retention. TDOC is well known for its land and expand strategy, and it has pretty much helped maintain a dominant position in the telemedicine space. The company does get criticized for not developing its own products but rather acquiring products with a proven PMF. As long as this is done in a financially efficient manner, I'm okay with that.

BetterHelp Transition to B2B

BetterHelp is the segment of the business, in my opinion, that is stifling the growth of TDOC, it still operates as a B2C business where patients directly pay for therapy sessions on the platform. Meanwhile, IC increased revenue from $374 million to $384 million on a year-to-year basis, BetterHelp experienced a decline from $286 million to $257 million in the same period.

To change this situation, management is working towards transitioning BetterHelp to be under an insurance coverage. This will reduce costs for patients and bring recurring revenue to the platform. BetterHelp is not just hurting the revenue growth of the business but also the profitability. As of the last quarter, TDOC OPEX margins accounted for about 78% of revenue, while gross margins were 71%. This is due to BetterHelp's high customer acquisition costs; the company is spending the majority of its operational expenses on marketing and advertising.

Q3 Earnings results

Income Statement

TDOC's Q3 revenue was $641 million, a 3% decline compared to Q3 2023, beating analysts' estimates by $8.5 million. The decline was mainly caused by a 10% decrease in BetterHelp's revenue, while IC revenue increased by 3%, and Adjusted EBITDA decreased by 6%. Management has stated it aims to reduce their OPEX costs as a way to increase the profitability of the company operationally. The company reduced their R&D spending by 14%, SG&A by 4% and other expenses by 5%, which brought a total reduction of 3% in operational expenses and resulted in a 31% decrease in Operational losses.

For the full-year guidance, the company guided for a low to mid-single digit growth in revenue, a mid-point of 15% in EBITDA margins and a mid-point of 94 million in IC members. The company did not provide any guidance for BetterHelp since the Q2 earnings of 2024.

Cashflow Statement

Even though TDOC is operationally unprofitable, it is profitable on a cash flow basis. The reason for the operational unprofitability is the D&A charges, which accounted for more than 80% of operational cash flow in Q3 last year. However, looking at their cashflow statement we can see that the company is generating good cashflow, it generated $110 million in CFO and $108.6 million in FCF when excluding growth capex (Purchase of intangibles) and SBC. Note: How I calculated my adjusted FCF is by deducting SBC since I consider it a real expense for shareholders. I came to a figure of $45 million in adjusted FCF. When you add back SBC, Free Cash Flow to the firm is $79 million as stated by the company.

Balance Sheet

Looking at the balance sheet, the first thing to point out is the $550 million Livongo convertible notes that mature in June. The conversion price for these notes is around $70 per share ($1000 / 13.94), which is far out-of-money from the current trading price of $14.33. Most likely; these notes will not convert to equity, but I cannot assure that. Also, TDOC has enough cash to pay back these notes in case they don't convert. This is an impressive balance sheet, with $1.6 billion in current assets and $1.9 billion in non-current assets, bringing a total of $3.5 billion in assets vs. $2 billion in liabilities. It really doesn't matter what happens to these convertible notes; the balance sheet will take care of itself.

Company Valuation

TDOC remains attractive for value investors that see a beaten-down stock ready for a reversal. The FWD Price/Cashflow ratio is trading at a 39% discount while the FWD Price/Sales ratio trades at a 74% discount relative to its sector even after a 50% rally since the beginning of this year.

Upcoming Earnings call

On the 26th of Feb, the company will be announcing their Q4 and full-year 2024 earnings results. I am looking forward to hearing more details about how the company is looking to integrate Catapult Health into their existing services; they did touch on that when they announced the news, but a detailed explanation would help in trying to gauge how that could boost IC revenues. I'm also interested in learning more about the AI product they announced. Is it going to be a new product segment they'd be selling to healthcare institutions, or is it going to be infused into the existing services they offer, and if so, how will it be monetized? This will give us a clear view of what could be a growth catalyst for TDOC.

Conclusion

I personally tend to watch for other qualitative metrics, growth in this case, to develop enough confidence to warrant a buy. I am not really a growth investor or a value investor. I'm more of a... let's say, hybrid investor. I look for value opportunities with a catalyst that might result in growth, and TDOC is not giving me the confidence as of yet. The company still has to prove it has the capabilities to successfully transition BetterHelp to be a B2B service since they don't show any interest in selling this part of the business. Once I see growth in revenue, I might warrant it a buy; for now, I will give it a hold rating.

data.title: VYM: A Moderate Growth Fund That Has Some Flaws
data.description:
Investors often have to make sacrifices when choosing how to best achieve their long-term objectives. Those with more aggressive goals will often have to take more risk, while even people seeking income in a low rate environment may have to move into equities out of bonds to get the dividend they desire.

The most common form of investing is to allocate capital to the indexes. The S&P 500 (SPY) and Nasdaq (NDAQ) have performed very well over the last several decades, and many people with limited time and resources will find investing in exchange-traded funds and other indexed investments more appealing.

ETF's have become an increasingly appealing way for many individuals to invest in these indexes, and there are a number of fund that are also closely tied to the benchmarks as well.

One exchange-traded fund that is correlated at a nearly 93 percent rate to the S&P 500 is the Vanguard High Dividend Yield Index ETF Shares (NYSEARCA:VYM). This investment has predictably performed reasonably well since the fund's inception date in November of 2006.

VYM has offered investors total returns of nearly 450 percent over the last 19 years. Still, the S&P 500 this fund's performance is heavily correlated with, has offered investors total returns of nearly 624 percent during the same time period.

Today, I wanted to update my coverage of VYM since I last wrote about this ETF in March of 2024. I am reiterating my hold rating. I rated the fund a hold nearly a year ago primarily because I did not believe this investment would outperform the S&P 500 or the broader indexes that this investment is heavily correlated to. Since I last analyzed VYM, the fund has offered investors total returns of 13.70 percent, while the S&P 500 has offered investors total returns of 16.53 percent during this same time period. VYM is likely to continue to pay out minimal income, and the fund's risk-reward profiler remains similar to the S&P 500 index the Vanguard investment continues to underperform. This ETF also has some core holdings in the health care industry have also consistently underperformed.

VYM HAS an expense ratio of .06 percent, a yield of 2.61 percent, and $75.86 billion in assets under management. The fund's holdings are 23.55 percent financials, 13.69 percent technology, 12.37 percent consumer defensives, 11.49 percent health care, 10.96 percent industrials, 9.17 percent energy, 7.38 percent consumer cyclical, 6.35 percent utilities, 2.98 percent communication, 2.05 percent basic materials, and .02 percent real estate. This is a large-cap fund, the average company has a market cap of nearly $150 billion. This is a well-diversified fund, VYM's four largest holdings, Broadcom (AVGO) Inc, Exxon Mobil (XOM), JPMorgan Chase & Co (JPM), and Walmart (WMT), only make-up a combined near 15 percent of the ETF.

VYM has a 93 percent correlation with the S&P 500, the main difference between this fund and the index is that this Vanguard investment has more exposure to conservative consumer defensive and technology sectors, and is more overweight than the financial sector. The primary reason VYM and consistently and significantly underperformed the S&P 500 is because the fund has nearly 20 percent fewer holdings in the technology sector, an industry that has been a market leader for much of the last 19 years. Some of this Vanguard's fund's core health care holdings, such as Merck (MRK) and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) have underperformed for an extended period of time as well. Both of these companies are in the S&P 500.

VYM is a moderate growth fund, this ETF has paid out very minimal income over the last 2 decades, and the Vanguard fund isn't likely to pay out more in dividends moving forward. The energy sector is the only industry that this fund is overweight that consistently payouts out higher levels of income. The two sectors VYM is most leveraged to, the technology and financial industry, have traditionally paid out less in dividends. The new Dodd-Frank legislation also put stricter limits on banks' ability to pay out income to shareholders as well since many of these institutions have to maintain higher levels of capital.

VYM's year-end yield has been below 4 percent in 8 of the last 10 years. The fund has raised the dividend. The ETF has raised the dividend at nearly 5-7 percent a year over the last decade. This is a growth fund that will not be appealing to most income investors. VYM has also not significantly raised dividend payouts even when inflation rates accelerated in early 2021, the income paid out by this Vanguard fund is not indexed to inflation or the rising cost of living.

VYM will likely continue to significantly underperform the S&P 500 since this fund has significantly less exposure to the technology sector than the broader index in addition to have much more exposure to the more conservative consumer defensive sector. While this Vanguard fund would likely slightly outperform the broader index if a market-wide sell-off occurred, or if the technology sector were to underperform the rest of the index for an extended period of time, VYM is constructed as a growth fund to maximize total returns, not a defensive investment to minimize risk. This Vanguard fund has a very similar risk profile as the S&P 500 despite consistently offering investors inferior returns as well. The standard deviation of VYM over the last 10 years has been 15.74 percent while the standard deviation of the S&P 500 has historically been nearly 17 percent.

VYM has performed reasonably well since the fund's inception nearly 19 years ago, and this investment will likely still have some appeal to moderate growth investors. Still, this Vanguard fund is highly unlikely to outperform the S&P 500 that this ETF is correlated to at a 93 percent rate, and this investment should also continue to payout only modest income. While VYM may continue to offer investors a respectable return on investment, this fund's risk-reward profile will still make this Vanguard fund unappealing to most investors seeking to maximize overall returns.

data.title: Global Economic Outlook: February 2025
data.description:
Shifts in US policy, and their potential economic implications, remain in focus. Early February's ﬂurry of tariff-related announcements rattled global ﬁnancial markets initially. Pausing the proposed tariffs on US imports from Canada and Mexico subsequently soothed those initial jitters. Still, the surge in policy-related uncertainty has the capacity to cause signiﬁcant economic damage. We have factored rising US tariffs and related counter-measures into our forecasts since December 2024, with trade weakness the primary cause of the projected slowdown in quarter-over-quarter global real GDP growth rates this year. Weaker investment is a key downside risk.

Our global growth forecasts for 2025-26 have been lifted slightly in the February update, although this masks some contrasting national revisions. Although we still expect a slowdown in the US, our annual real GDP growth forecast for 2025 has been revised upward to reﬂect still solid momentum around the turn of the year, particularly in consumer spending. Some headwinds to US growth are intensifying, including higher-than-expected Treasury yields and private borrowing costs, and the related strength of the US dollar. Japan's higher 2025 growth forecast largely reﬂects upward revisions to 2024 data.

Real GDP growth (% change)

World

North America

Other Americas

Western Europe

Emerging Europe

Asia-Pacific

Mideast- N. Africa

Sub-Saharan Africa

Data compiled Feb. 17, 2025. Source: S&P Global Market Intelligence. © 2025 S&P Global.

We have revised down our real GDP growth forecasts for 2025 in Brazil, the UK and Russia. The downward revisions to Brazil's forecasts, for this year and next, reﬂect much tighter monetary policy than previously forecast. Recent business surveys, including S&P Global's Purchasing Managers Indexes™ (PMIs®), are already showing signs of pronounced weakness in services. The reduction to 2025's UK growth forecast reﬂects a continuation of the recent run of weak GDP prints and the expected effects of tighter ﬁscal policy. Tight monetary policy and weaker export prospects contribute to the lower growth forecast for Russia.

January's global PMI® ﬁgures have already signaled a loss of growth momentum. The global composite output index fell to its lowest level for a year and is indicative of below-potential global real GDP growth. Business activity in services fell to its lowest level since late 2023. While the indications for global manufacturing and trade have perked up a little over recent months, they remain comparatively weak. February's "ﬂash" PMI® ﬁgures, released from the 20th onward, will be the ﬁrst gauge of the fall-out from the surge in policy-related uncertainty.

Monetary policy divergence is set to continue in the near term. We now forecast that the US Federal Reserve will lower policy rates by only 25 basis points this year, in May. Even this call, which is driven by the monetary policy stance remaining somewhat restrictive, looks marginal. In contrast, given relatively weak eurozone economic conditions, the European Central Bank is expected to be in the vanguard of further easing in 2025. Recent modest rate cuts in various emerging economies, including India and South Africa, signal a more cautious approach, with the strength of the US dollar a constraint. Brazil's consecutive 100 basis point rate hikes tell their own story, meanwhile, with more of the same expected in the period ahead.

Consumer price inflation (% annual change)

Recent signals for inﬂation have been less favorable. Consumer price inﬂation rates for services generally remain rather sticky. The rate which we calculate and track for the G5 group of economies has maintained its very gradual downward trend, edging down from 4.2% to 4.1% in December 2024. Even in economies with comparatively weak demand, such as the eurozone and UK, services inﬂation rates remain around or above 4%. Services price data from our global PMIs® picked up markedly in January, with input and output price indexes reaching their highest levels in almost a year. With core goods inﬂation now picking up, albeit from very low levels, stickiness in services and overall core consumer price inﬂation rates could constrain the room for policy rate cuts, compounding growth headwinds.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: BXSL Vs. MSDL: MSDL Is A Better Buy Right Now
data.description:
Blackstone Secured Lending (NYSE:BXSL) and Morgan Stanley Direct Lending (NYSE:MSDL) are comparable business development companies. Both BDCs share an investment strategy with first-lien debt at the forefront of their portfolios, invest in US middle-market businesses, are susceptible to the changing interest rate environment, etc. What's more? They both share a space in my portfolio.

I had the pleasure to cover both BXSL and MSDL in the past. Should you be interested in taking a better look at the development of my views on both companies, here for BXSL or here for MSDL.

It is often the case that when two different companies share many similarities, a question arises: Which one is a better buy? I examined that and would like to share my thoughts in this article—let me tell you this: I'm currently buying only one of these two defensive debt investors.

BXSL vs MSDL: Which one is a better buy?

Investment Focus, Portfolio Structure, And Key Metrics Comparison

Let's approach it by comparing some key metrics in terms of portfolio composition, investments, portfolio companies, non-accruals, and other positions that will help us with the investment decision. What's the best way to make it transparent and easily comparable? Share it through the table.

BXSL MSDL
Portfolio structure Debt-oriented Debt-oriented
First-lien debt share in portfolio 98.7% 96%
Number of portfolio companies 252 200
Borrower's concentration (Top 10 borrowers) 25% 16.6%
The share in the portfolio of the largest borrower 3% 2.1%
Non-accruals (at cost) 0.2% 0.2%
EBITDA of portfolio companies (weighted average) $194m $144.9m
Weighted average loan-to-value 46.5% 40%
Floating-rated investments 98.8% 99.6%

We'll get to the valuation, dividend coverage, and dividend yield later. For now, let's focus on the abovementioned metrics. As I mentioned earlier, both BDCs share many similarities with both not only debt-oriented but also first-lien debt oriented (with BXSL having slightly higher exposure), impressive number of portfolio companies, low portfolio concentration (it's worth pointing out that MSDL is better diversified in this area), and same non-accrual status (at cost).

While MSDL's portfolio performance is slightly more susceptible to interest rate changes (as it holds a higher percentage of floating-rated investments in the portfolio structure), both BDCs interest income depends heavily on interest rate fluctuations.

We can easily conclude that BXSL and MSDL share a lot of similarities and are two of the most defensive BDCs in the market, featuring a dominant exposure to first-lien debt, which is the safest form of providing financing as it ensures priority in the repayment ladder in the case of a potential insolvency/default of a portfolio company.

Which BDC Offers Better Dividend Coverage?

As we established earlier, BXSL and MSDL are highly susceptible to interest rate changes. While we won't be able to quantify each effect of potential interest rate decreases (e.g., the likelihood of improved deal flow), we can examine the impact on the cost of debt and interest income from debt investments.

I based my calculations on the following assumptions:

  1. I focused on regular dividend payments.
  2. I included the impact of both the cost of debt and interest income.
  3. I used the data for the last twelve months ending September 30, 2024 (the latest available) for both BDCs as a base for Net Investment Income (NII) estimation.
  4. I adjusted the abovementioned NII by the impact of FED's interest rate from September 2024 (50 bps).
  5. Finally, I estimated the regular DPS coverage of BXSL and MSDL based on different scenarios regarding interest rate levels.

You're probably wondering what the result of the analysis is, right? I won't keep you waiting. Please review the tables below.

Hopefully, you can see that these are easily comparable. Please note that calculated coverages include each (known as of the time of writing this article) market information or financial data published by either of these BDCs.

The conclusion is quite simple: despite all the love I have for BXSL, MSDL currently offers better regular DPS coverage (116% vs 105%) and is set to withstand potential further cuts better (e.g. 94% vs 83% for further 150 cps cut).

The Largest Difference Between BXSL and MSDL Lies In Valuation

I won't sugar-coat it. BXSL trades at a significant premium when compared to MSDL, which barely trades at pair with it Net Asset Value. Don't get me wrong, I 100% believe that BXSL deserves a premium to its NAV given its quality, reliability, and attractive distributions. However... having in mind the scope of similarities MSDL shares with BXSL and also some advantages it has over it (e.g., regular DPS coverage), shouldn't MSDL trade at a higher P/BV multiple? I believe it should.

Once the market appreciates the quality and solid income MSDL brings to the investment table, Morgan Stanley Direct Lending shall benefit from a greater ability to gather capital. Why is that? - since the higher the premium, the more proceeds can a given BDC generate through issuing equity. Therefore, BXSL can currently raise capital more efficiently when compared to MSDL, but as an investor raised on the timeless wisdom of value investing, I see a greater window of opportunity in MSDL.

My Conclusion, Your Takeaway: I Own Both, But I Am Reinvesting In Just MSDL

I've been more than happy with BXSL's performance in my portfolio. I don't often require much capital appreciation when investing in BDCs, as I treat them mostly as income-producing holdings. My MSDL investments haven't been as rewarding (so far), but regarding current prices and the state of both businesses, I see a more attractive risk-to-reward ratio in MSDL.

MSDL is substantially cheaper, offers slightly higher regular DPS yield (9.5% vs BXSL's 8.9%), and shares BXSL's defensive nature, showcasing first-lien orientation, low non-accruals, and portfolio diversification, and should be capable of withstanding more interest rate cuts than BXSL.

After taking it all into account, I decided to seize further reinvestments on behalf of BXSL (I rate BXSL a 'hold') and continue reinvesting in MSDL (I rate uphold my 'buy' rating for MSDL). Although both BDCs can experience some struggles in the upcoming quarters with potentially decreasing interest rate environments, uncertain economic environment, and the ongoing risk of rising non-accruals (as many businesses operate at a tighter and tighter liquidity), I will gladly hold on to both of them and gradually increase my exposure to MSDL. Thank you!

data.title: The State Of Fixed Income In 2025
data.description:
By Michael Goosay, Chief Investment Officer, Global Fixed Income

Transcript

Michael Goosay - Well, if you understand fixed income, it's all about future value of today's dollar. And as yield investors are focused on maximizing the amount of income that they get in their fixed income allocation, future inflation erodes the purchasing power of that future income payment that they would receive from investing in the fixed income markets.

Inflation has been higher than investors expected, given the amount of stimulus that was pushed through the economy during the beginning of the COVID outbreak. You had significant amount of fiscal stimulus through the form of cash being pushed back into the consumer, spending at the federal government and state government levels, as well as monetary policy which was targeting zero interest rates, driving meaningful amounts of demand as all this money sloshed around the system.

The idea of having more demand than you had supply, whether that was related to supply chain issues or just people that felt wealthier because of the amount of money that was in the system, ultimately drove inflation to higher rates than we had seen since the 1970s or early 1980s. Since then, you've seen that money be largely spent. A lot of those checks that were sent out to the consumer have already been spent in the economy.

Monetary policy has been adjusted by the Fed through hiking interest rates to 5% to 5.25% where we sat until earlier this year. And the result is that inflation has shown improvement. Maybe not down yet to the Fed's target level, but it still remains in a positive trajectory and one that we're comfortable will be ultimately achieved over time.

There is a target from the Federal Reserve to have stable prices and full employment. Now, we have used 2% as that definition of stable prices for quite some time. The Fed talks about it as being their ultimate North Star, their goal. But it's not necessarily a hard and fast rule that they have to hit 2% at all times.

And so you are going to go through periods like we think we're experiencing now, where inflation could be in the neighborhood of 2%. Maybe not exactly at 2%. And it will still allow the Fed to adjust monetary policy to more of a neutral policy rate, as they aim to keep that secondary objective that they have, which is full employment. As 2025 unfolds, we expect that inflation will show further signs of improvement as some of these lingering issues that have overhung the labor market are resolving themselves.

Current yield environment is one that is pretty average from a historical perspective. But if you compare it to where we were just a few years ago when rates were close to zero, it is certainly easier or with taking less risk in order to achieve an income target that an investor may be looking to enjoy.

We think today that the best opportunities are still in some of the spread sectors. High yield bonds offer attractive risk-reward characteristics. Yields are north of 7%. The quality of the high yield universe of issuers is higher than it's been in the lifetime of the index, and we continue to see improvement in the underlying fundamentals of these companies that are issuing in that market. So we believe the high yield market offers real attractive risk-reward characteristics today.

Similarly, we look at even investment-grade corporates as well as municipal bonds as offering attractive yields in an environment where we expect that the quality of those issuers will remain robust.

Most of the time, when you go through an economic cycle like we just had, you see that eventually you'll get to the point where inflation does come down towards that 2% target. And it's generally in an environment where the unemployment rate is rising. The labor market is starting to show signs of deterioration.

At this point, we're still seeing some of the volatility in the markets attributable to the unknowns of how the impact of COVID will affect long-term inflation rates; the labor market; globalization trends that had been firmly in place. And so, as a portfolio manager within the fixed income markets, it's still a little challenging to try to find exactly what the forward-looking path should be for interest rates. And so, as a result, we think it's really important to focus on high quality companies, high quality investments that offer that safe, secure income in the fixed income markets.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: FTLS: This Long-Short ETF Is Still A Buy
data.description:
Thesis

With equities seemingly making new highs every day, investors might ask themselves if the asset class is not overvalued. While it is almost impossible to call a top, there are strategies one can implement in order to manage risk. Managing your downside is a tool that retail investors should use rather than trying to time the market. We have covered in the past the First Trust Long/Short Equity ETF (FTLS), a fund which has delivered since our rating:

The vehicle is up +27.8% since our article, trailing the S&P 500 indeed but posting a very healthy result with a low volatility profile. In today's article we are going to re-visit the ETF and articulate for investors why we believe the name to still represent an opportune 'Buy' when swapping out other passive market ETFs.

Active fund with a long/short mandate

This ETF is an active one, and aims to generate long term total returns via long and short positions in individual equities:

Seeks to provide investors with long term total return. The Fund intends to pursue its investment objective by establishing long and short positions in a portfolio of Equity Securities. FTLS aims to keep pace with the S&P 500 Index over a full market cycle, with less volatility. FTLS may lag the index in up markets, but historically has outperformed in down markets.

The fund is currently net long via its positioning:

The long exposure is +98%, while the short is at -33.3%. More interestingly let us look at how the sectoral exposures shape up:

The fund is net long Information Technology and Financials as top sectors, while almost net flat for Industrials (which is a top shorted sector). Materials for example is a sector where the ETF is net short (if we add the +1.9% long position with the -2.8% net short we get a negative figure).

Since this ETF has been outstanding during many market cycles, we are not going to comment on our view on their net positioning, just observe where the concentration resides.

For long/short names it is almost impossible to predict future performance from a snapshot of holdings because the manager actively changes the portfolio. An investor needs to understand that the portfolio manager can go long Materials tomorrow if it chooses to do so via their market insight. The best way to gauge composition is via long-term performance and analytics. Good managers tend to post very robust long-term results with attractive analytics.

The volatility profile is key

What we like most about this fund is its volatility profile. While long-only names tend to have deep drawdowns and high standard deviations, well-run long/short funds exhibit really robust analytics:

The ETF has a standard deviation which is half of what the index exhibits, and a beta-factor which is also roughly 50% of the index. Kindly remember that low beta names are the ones that tend to have much lower drawdowns in a market move lower.

The fund's decisions are well encapsulated by the Sharpe ratio which is much higher than the one exhibited by the index. As an investor, you want the highest possible Sharpe for a fund. The name also presents an 'Alpha' ratio which is defined as the outperformance on a risk-adjusted basis versus the benchmark. The high correlation factor shows us that the name is not long only defensive stocks, but actually builds a portfolio similar to the index, but takes long positions in the stronger names only.

Let us look at how the name has done versus the index in the past three years:

Please note that on a 3-year basis, the fund's drawdown was a mere -5% versus -18% for the S&P 500 (SPX) as mirrored by the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). The data for a 3-year look back starts in late February 2022, thus the full drawdown for 2022 that you will see on other websites does not marry the above graph for the index.

Not only did FTLS have a very shallow drawdown, but the fund participated in most of the upside as exhibited by the index, and had a higher total return versus the index for most of the analyzed period (i.e. the teal line is above the orange line for most of the time-frame).

How to fight high valuation metrics

Again, as stated in the 'Thesis' section it is almost impossible to time the market. However, what we can do is recognize when equities are cheap versus when they are expensive:

The current forward P/E for the SPX is over 22x, toward the top of the historic range. While we are not saying that we will have a -30% correction tomorrow, history tends to show that periods of very high P/E ratios are followed by normalizations. And usually the normalizations come from the lowering of the 'P' (i.e. price) in the equation, not by the bumping-up of the 'E' (i.e. earnings).

We can see from the above graph that we are not at the absolute historic high in P/E-s here, and thus we can still move higher. However, eventually, a normalization will occur. We are of the opinion that investors who will try to call the top and suddenly switch to cash at the very top of the market will be quite rare. Most people will be caught off-guard and will end up incurring losses if they sell at the bottom.

A better strategy, in our opinion, is to switch from outright SPY to instruments like FTLS which do capture most of the upside but have very limited drawdown profiles. This fund has proven itself historically, so an investor can have a high degree of confidence that drawdowns will be within certain historic ranges, and negative total returns will be limited:

The fund posted only a -4.8% return in 2018, and only -5.5% in 2022. Those are incredible figures if you think about it. In a year like 2022 when the SPY ended up at -18%, FTLS was down a mere -5.5%. There is no 'secret sauce' here that represents a black box. The fund managers are very good at identifying strong and weak names from the overall population, and very good at establishing relative value trades where one can be long one name in the industrials space, for example, and short a different one and the same sector. This trading strategy ensures there are no losses when the entire sector moves in a certain direction, while isolating the very factors that make a stock outperform.

Conclusion

FTLS is a long/short ETF. The name has posted outstanding results in the past three years, with low beta figures and a low standard deviation. The name had a shallow drawdown in 2022 and mirrored most of the SPY upside afterwards. We like this fund's composition and managers and believe it represents a robust alternative to an outright SPY positioning. While we cannot time the market, we can observe a very high P/E ratio, and are of the opinion that defensive retail investors would do well to swap from SPY into FTLS.

data.title: Where To Look When Equities Are Priced For Exceptionalism
data.description:
As U.S. equity valuations hover near historical highs, it's a good time for investors to evaluate the sustainability of stock gains and to revisit the fundamental benefits of portfolio diversification.

Asset prices are "elevated by many metrics right now," Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell said at a 29 January policy press conference. Two days earlier, a front-page story in The Wall Street Journal, titled "Premium for Owning Stocks Vs. Bonds Disappears," noted that the equity risk premium - defined as the gap between the S&P 500's earnings yield and the 10-year Treasury yield - had turned negative for the first time since 2002.

Another common equity gauge, the cyclically adjusted price-to-earnings (CAPE) ratio, has climbed to levels previously seen only twice in the past three decades: during the dot-com bubble and the post-pandemic recovery (see Figure 1). Those prior CAPE peaks occurred when Fed projections and consensus market forecasts called for U.S. growth of 3.5% to 4.7% annually. Today, however, growth is forecast to be only about 2% for 2025.

Figure 1: CAPE ratio is near peak levels

It seems especially concerning that valuations are so stretched when uncertainty is so high (see Figure 2), with U.S. tariff policies set to reshape the global economic landscape (for more, see our latest Cyclical Outlook, "Uncertainty Is Certain").

Figure 2: Trade policy uncertainty has surged

The case for continued U.S. stock market gains appears to rely on two key assumptions: that "this time is different," and that U.S. economic exceptionalism - outperformance versus the rest of the world - will endure. These assumptions were tested in late January when fears about the durability of artificial intelligence investments, amid rising competition from China, sparked a sell-off. Such volatility could become more common, and a negative equity risk premium may not provide adequate compensation.

Diversification is essential

Investors should revisit a time-tested strategy: diversification. The equity risk premium has turned negative partly because bond yields have reached the most attractive levels in years (see Figure 3).

Figure 3: Bond yields have risen above S&P earnings yield

The recent reemergence of the traditional negative correlation between stocks and bonds further enhances bonds as a counterbalance to equity exposure (for more, see our November 2024 publication, "Negative Correlations, Positive Allocations").

Over the long term, U.S. equities are essential components of any investment portfolio. Equity investments have helped power the growth of the U.S. economy while creating wealth and retirement security for millions of investors. However, periods of elevated valuations have tended to heighten shorter-term risks to the equity outlook.

As Fed Chair Powell also noted on 29 January, it's human nature to underestimate tail risks. Following outsize stock market gains in recent years, which may have tilted portfolio allocations even further toward equities, investors should evaluate the risks related to valuations and remember the importance of bonds in a balanced portfolio. Given the historical tendency for valuations to revert to the mean, it could be a mistake to assume that today's exceptional conditions will last indefinitely.

Disclosures

All investments contain risk and may lose value. Investing in the bond market is subject to risks, including market, interest rate, issuer, credit, inflation risk, and liquidity risk. The value of most bonds and bond strategies are impacted by changes in interest rates. Bonds and bond strategies with longer durations tend to be more sensitive and volatile than those with shorter durations; bond prices generally fall as interest rates rise, and low interest rate environments increase this risk. Reductions in bond counterparty capacity may contribute to decreased market liquidity and increased price volatility. Bond investments may be worth more or less than the original cost when redeemed. Equities may decline in value due to both real and perceived general market, economic and industry conditions.

Diversification does not ensure against loss.

Statements concerning financial market trends or portfolio strategies are based on current market conditions, which will fluctuate. There is no guarantee that these investment strategies will work under all market conditions or are appropriate for all investors and each investor should evaluate their ability to invest for the long term, especially during periods of downturn in the market. Outlook and strategies are subject to change without notice.

PIMCO as a general matter provides services to qualified institutions, financial intermediaries and institutional investors. Individual investors should contact their own financial professional to determine the most appropriate investment options for their financial situation. This material contains the opinions of the manager and such opinions are subject to change without notice. This material has been distributed for informational purposes only and should not be considered as investment advice or a recommendation of any particular security, strategy or investment product. Information contained herein has been obtained from sources believed to be reliable, but not guaranteed. No part of this material may be reproduced in any form, or referred to in any other publication, without express written permission. PIMCO is a trademark of Allianz Asset Management of America LLC in the United States and throughout the world.

data.title: Tanger Inc. 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Tanger Inc. in conjunction with their 2024 Q4 earnings call.

data.title: Short-Term Energy Outlook, February 2025
data.description:
A guest post by D Coyne

The EIA Short-Term Energy Outlook (STEO) was published recently. An estimate for World C+C output from November 2024 to December 2026 is presented in the chart below. This estimate is based on crude oil estimates in the STEO for OPEC12 nations, OPEC+ minus OPEC12 nations, and US C+C estimates and the trend in the ratio of the STEO crude estimates and C+C estimates from the EIA's International Energy Statistics for OPEC12, OPEC+ minus OPEC12, and non-OPEC+ minus US C+C output from November 2018 to October 2024. For the next 4 charts, the horizontal and vertical scales are the same, with 9 years on the horizontal axis and 12000 kb/d from the lowest to highest values on the vertical axis, so the differences in rates of change are easier to compare. The thin line represents monthly data or forecasts after October 2024 and the markers with labels show the average annual output for each year.

Last month's estimates using a similar method for estimation had 2024 at 81,841 kb/d, 2025 at 83,176 kb/d, and 2026 at 84,420 kb/d, slightly lower than this month's STEO. Note that this STEO forecast is different from my forecast which is 82,640 kb/d in 2025 and 83,090 kb/d in 2026 for average annual World C+C output.

Last month's estimate for OPEC+ was 44,903 kb/d in 2024, 45,183 kb/d in 2025, and 46,082 kb/d in 2026. OPEC+ output increases at an annual rate of about 530 kb/d from 2024 to 2026.

The Non-OPEC+ minus US estimate in last month's report was 23723 kb/d in 2024, 24,449 kb/d in 2025, and 24,716 kb/d in 2026. The annual rate of increase from 2024 to 2026 for Non-OPEC+ minus US C+C is about 512 kb/d.

The US C+C estimate in last month's report was 13,214 kb/d in 2024, 13,544 kb/d in 2025, and 13,623 kb/d in 2026. The annual rate of increase from 2024 to 2026 for US C+C is about 259 kb/d. To get a feel for how the STEO estimate might change over time, I looked back at the US C+C STEO estimate from 12 months ago (estimate ended in 2025). The annual forecast for US C+C in that older report was about 100 kb/d lower than the current STEO forecast for both 2024 and 2025 even with higher oil price forecasts in the STEO from Feb 2024 compared with the current report. Natural gas prices were also forecast higher in 2024 in the Feb 2024 STEO, but 2025 Henry Hub spot prices were lower than the Feb 2025 STEO.

Note the big drop in oil prices forecast for 2026, this is the reason the EIA expects US C+C output will be nearly flat in 2026. The low oil prices are expected to lead to fewer new wells completed in tight oil plays and slower overall growth in tight oil, which is barely high enough to offset falling output elsewhere in the US. US annual average C+C output rises by 137 kb/d in 2026, less than half of the increase in 2025 (380 kb/d).

The high World liquids supply relative to World Consumption in 2025 and 2026 leads to stock builds, which drives down Brent oil prices from $77.13/b in 2025Q1 to $64/b in 2026Q4 (WTI falls from $73.62 to $60/b over the same period in the STEO forecast.)

The forecast above for Global liquids production looks very optimistic given the low oil prices in the forecast. Either output will be lower or prices will be higher than the STEO forecast, probably a bit of both.

The EIA expects demand for distillate fuel and jet fuel to increase in 2025 and 2026, while improved fleet efficiency for passenger vehicles leads to lower demand for gasoline. Low crude oil prices in 2026 leads to low growth in crude oil output.

Two planned refinery closures in the US (about 400 kb/d capacity combined) in 2025 and 2026 lead to lower refinery output in 2025 and 2026. This is offset by a lower refinery exports forecast for 2025 and 2026.

The higher natural gas prices in the forecast leads to higher US natural gas output and lower natural gas consumption in 2026. The share of natural gas used for electricity output falls from 43% in 2024 to 39% in 2026, in part due to higher natural gas prices.

The severe winter weather in 2025Q1 is expected to lead to large stock draws in natural gas.

Low stock levels of natural gas in 2025 and 2026 compared to the 5-year average leads to the forecast natural gas price increases.

While demand for electricity is forecast to increase in 2025 and 2026, the trend in coal consumption for electric power is a lower share, falling from 34% in 2015 to about 15% in 2026. Natural gas used for electric power saw an increase from about a 32% share in 2015 to 43% in 2024, which is forecast to fall to 39% in 2026. The overall share of coal and natural gas for US electric power output is forecast to fall from 66% in 2015 to 54% in 2026. Wind and solar power output is forecast to rise from a 6% share in 2015 to roughly a 19% share in 2026.

The rising real electricity prices in the US are due to needed grid modernization (see link for more detail).

On an annual basis, Permian output continues to rise from 2025 to 2026, but the monthly forecast is pretty flat in 2026, probably due to the low prices in the STEO forecast. Other parts of the US lower 48 excluding the Gulf of Mexico besides the Permian region see declining output in the second half of 2025 and most of 2026 (see chart below.)

Using Novi Data for Permian tight oil output and assuming conventional Permian region output is declining at about 3.5% per year, I get the following using EIA, RRC (TX), OCD (NM) data for the Permian region along with the Novi data through August 2023.

The estimate above only extends through October 2024 because, typically, there are future revisions to the most recent month estimate.

The chart above is in units of kb/d and represents Permian tight oil. The EIA STEO Regional Permian annual estimate had conventional output subtracted for 2025 (443 kbd) and 2026 (427 kb/d). For 2024, the STEO Permian regional estimates for November and December 2024 were used with conventional output subtracted (453 and 451 for Nov and Dec, respectively). The annual 2024 "STEO" estimate uses my Permian tight oil estimate for Jan to Oct 2024 along with the STEO Permian tight oil estimates from Nov and Dec 2024. The 2022 and 2023 annual "STEO" estimates are based on my Permian tight oil estimate (presented in previous chart) using TX, NM, EIA, and Novi data. The important differences are 2025 and 2026 where my estimate is considerably more conservative than the EIA estimate for Permian tight oil.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Expeditors: A Powerful Moat
data.description:
“The fundamental algorithm of life – repeat what works” – Charlie Munger

I recently wrote an article on Expeditors (NYSE:EXPD), highlighting my positive views on the company and their valuation. I believe the company has a moat as evidenced by their high and persistent ROE while using no leverage. ROE adjusted for cash, as I measure it, has averaged above 30% for the past 15 years. This is a remarkable feat.

The stock price has followed:

Over the last 30 years, the 80x return means the stock is close to becoming a 100-bagger. With a $15 billion market cap, operating in a fragmented industry, it should still have significant runway ahead.

Therefore, a key question going forward is whether such ROE performance is sustainable. The only way to answer this is to try and identify the source of the competitive advantage and assess how likely it is to persist for the next decade or two.

The source of the moat is not readily identifiable, however. I have owned this stock for the past decade. Management is non-promotional, there are no investor days or earnings presentations. The prevailing theory is that their network effect is what creates their moat.

This is for sure part of the story, and I don't disagree. With over $10 billion in globally diversified revenue, the company certainly has developed buying power from consolidating shipper demand through their four decades of operation.

The resulting vast network of service centers distributed globally means that each additional node makes the network more valuable to existing nodes, the classic network effect. Furthermore, replicating this scale can only be done gradually and at initial losses as the cost of IT development, training, etc. cannot be diluted through an installed base.

But I believe there is more than that. And the clues are hidden in plain sight. Few people really take the time to read a company’s proxy statements. And I don't blame them. They are typically remarkably boring; but essential if you are trying to understand management incentives.

Occasionally, a certain proxy stands out. This happened to me on Expeditors. Through the years, I have become convinced that their compensation structure is an equally relevant source of competitive advantage.

Because they need to disclose compensation structure and philosophy to the SEC at least yearly, we get perhaps more information than they would rather put out. But, given the divergence in their practices from those of their competitors - or the market in general, I have little worry that their plans can be successfully implemented elsewhere.

What they have built around their compensation practices has resulted in a unique situation. A sort of experiment that cannot be replicated unless it is adopted from the beginning. They tried a different system and have stuck to their guns. The fundamental policies around compensation have remained unchanged since 1985.

The compensation philosophy

Essentially, the philosophy attacks head-on the main problem with any compensation design. How to minimize agency cost. The way Expeditors designed their philosophy is both simple and powerful:

  • Pay all C-level executives the same salary at a way-below-market rate ($100,000/yr), and never rise it.
  • Tie almost all cash compensation to vary directly in line with an easy to understand metric aligned with shareholders – operating income.

As a result, during most years, greater than 80% of compensation is variable. The method of deploying this requires more detail than is probably interesting to readers. In short, they set up a bonus pool and fund it with 10% of operating income. All named executives and senior managers have a share – a pre-determined “stake” in the bonus pool that gets reviewed periodically by the compensation committee.

The underlying intent is twofold. First, it allows for straightforward alignment with shareholders, who benefit proportionally from the growth in operating income. Second, it allows for largely decentralized management of regional offices. These managers, in effect, are entrepreneurs, sharing directly from the outcomes of their own day-to-day decisions.

A further key feature includes a need to recoup prior losses in full - at the relevant regional site - in order to trigger future bonuses. This is important because it guards against excessive risk taking (for example, with extending credit to customers). Is this effective? I will quote from the proxy:

"The Company has never suffered an annual or quarterly operating loss since going public in September 1984" (page 29).

Alignment at work

The past few years give us an important “real life” test case of the compensation practices at work. Volumes surged during the pandemic at the same time capacity was constrained in supply chains. Expeditors enjoyed major revenue and income tailwinds during 2021 and 2022, which normalized in 2023.

Operating income increased to above $2 billion during 2022. By 2023, however, it had dropped 50% to around $1 billion.

Here is what happened to named executive compensation during this volatile period:

For both the CEO and CFO, compensation in 2023 decreased almost 50% from 2021, in line with results. Same with other three named executives. Their reward is in lockstep with shareholder's experience.

As for the CEO, he has been with the company for 42 years - and just this week announced his retirement. His successor is another long time executive steeped in the culture. The compensation plan clearly reinforces the goals of alignment and accountability. It also seems to attract and retain people that like to see direct links between input and output. Senior executive average tenure, according to the company, is 32 years.

And this is a people business. Systems are also important, but people drive logistics, especially in asset-light outfits like Expeditors. It comes down to the thousands of decisions made by people in front of customers and suppliers every day. Prospective employees can opt in (or not) to this compensation scheme. But no competitor can re-jigger their own plans to such an extent without suffering potentially existential disruption.

Risks going forward

As referenced, no debt is a wonderful thing. Not only does it make default impossible, but the company is also able to withstand volatility in the business cycle without laying off people. It did not lay anyone off when the pandemic started, while others did, and was ready to service the unexpected surge that followed.

Trade flows will fluctuate regardless of tariffs. Which goods get manufactured here or there, content rules, de-minimis, etc., all create complexity. And greater complexity means more opportunity for Expeditors to offer its clients expertise.

The one risk that should worry investors is cyber risk. Attacking supply chains goes in and out of vogue. Expeditors was already a victim some three years ago and is still ramping up its IT security (as evidenced by the growth in its IT staff headcount).

And the cost is larger than the increased headcount. After having to unplug clients from its systems for several weeks back then, some customers realized their dependency on Expeditors and have decided to carry a second service provider as precaution. A long-term impact that will take many years of flawless security to dissolve.

Recent results and valuation

Expeditors reported Q4 earnings this week. EPS of $1.68 was similar to Q3 EPS of $1.63 and volumes continued to recover in both air freight and ocean container. At this pace, it looks to easily surpass expectations of around $5/share for 2025.

Regarding valuation, the stock currently trades around $115. Take away around $10/share in cash, and the PE multiple (ex-cash) is close to 20x. If you believe, like me, that 2025 earnings will tend toward $6, then the forward PE is already below 20x, a wonderful price to pay for a low-risk compounder such as this.

Conclusion

Expeditors enjoys a powerful combination of competitive advantages. The culture and alignment created by the compensation practices cannot be “adopted” by a competitor with similar scale and network. Only an early-stage competitor can hope to possibly adopt similar compensation practices, but would have to work many years to build a network.

Therefore, what we have in front of us is a robust moat. One built year by year, growing the network organically while promoting entrepreneurial spirit and shareholder alignment through an "unusually variable" compensation scheme. In other words, a moat that was built by following the principle quoted at the beginning of the article; they found something that worked – 40 years ago – and repeated.
